All Questions
Were You Asked This?
Design a fast food restaurant chain management system

Design a system for a global fast food restaurant chain that manages location-based menus with different items and pricing, and displays visually appealing menus on in-store panels.

Asked at:

Google

Salesforce

A fast food restaurant chain management system is a content and menu orchestration platform for global quick-service restaurants. Corporate and store teams define menus, localized pricing, and promotional content, then publish visually appealing playlists to in-store digital panels that must update quickly and keep working even if the network is flaky. Think of the digital menu boards you see at McDonald's or Starbucks that change by time of day, show local prices, and rotate promotions.

Interviewers ask this to test your ability to design at the intersection of configuration management, real-time distribution, and offline-first edge devices. They expect you to reason about hierarchical overrides (corporate → region → store), pushing updates to tens of thousands of endpoints, distributing large media assets efficiently, and using context signals (weather, store load) for smart promotions while maintaining safety and control. Strong answers demonstrate clear scoping, pragmatic consistency choices, and a rollout/operability mindset.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to define and manage menus and localized prices per location, with time-based schedules (e.g., breakfast, lunch) and effective dates.

Users should be able to create and schedule visually rich content (images/videos/layouts) for specific panels, stores, and dayparts, and preview what each panel will show.

Users should be able to target promotions based on context (e.g., weather, time, store occupancy) with guardrails and overrides.

Users should be able to publish updates to selected stores/panels with near real-time propagation, safe rollbacks, and audit/version history.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How do you push near real-time updates to tens of thousands of panels worldwide while ensuring reliability and cost control?

How will you deliver large media assets (images/videos) efficiently and ensure panels always have something to show, even offline?

How do you model hierarchical menus and pricing with overrides and schedules while keeping reads fast and consistent enough for stores?

How will you use context (weather, inventory, store occupancy) to drive smart promotions without causing instability or bad customer experiences?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Digital panels need to reflect menu, price, and promotion changes within seconds across a global fleet. A real-time updates pattern (push over WebSocket/MQTT plus periodic reconcile) ensures low-latency propagation with resilience to flaky networks.

Read More
Handling Large Blobs

Menus are visually rich and depend on images and videos. Treating media as large blobs served via CDN and cached on devices separates heavy content delivery from small control messages, improving reliability and cost.

Read More
Scaling Reads

The system is read-heavy at the edge: thousands of panels repeatedly fetch resolved menus and playlists. Precomputing store-specific views and caching aggressively ensures fast, predictable reads at scale.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

A durable, scalable event backbone for publish/subscribe of configuration changes, device heartbeats, and contextual signals (e.g., weather updates). Kafka enables fanout, replay for troubleshooting, and integration with downstream processors.

Read More
Redis

A low-latency cache for resolved store menus/playlists and device session state at regional edges. Redis helps panels fetch read-optimized documents quickly and supports rate limiting and feature flags during rollouts.

Read More
DynamoDB

A globally scalable key-value/document store well-suited for hierarchical configuration, versioned bundles, and per-store effective menus with predictable performance. DynamoDB’s partitioning and TTL features fit multi-tenant, read-heavy workloads.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Dropbox

Like syncing files to many devices, this system must distribute and version large media assets and ensure offline availability with conflict-free rollbacks and integrity checks.

Read
Practice
FB News Feed

Both require assembling a personalized view (here: per-store resolved menu and targeted promotions) from multiple sources and delivering low-latency, read-optimized content at massive scale.

Read
Practice
FB Live Comments

Real-time fanout to many clients, connection lifecycle management, and backpressure concerns mirror the need to push control updates and promotions to thousands of panels quickly and reliably.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Ignoring offline-first requirements for panels

Assuming constant connectivity leads to blank or outdated screens during network issues. Panels must cache assets and a last-known-good playlist, with deterministic rollback and time-based cutovers handled locally.

Treating large media assets like regular API payloads

Pushing images/videos through the control plane or the database causes high latency, cost, and failures. Assets should be content-addressed, served via CDN, and pre-staged on devices with integrity checks.

Global strong consistency for all menu/price writes

Coordinating cross-region transactions for every change adds latency and fragility. Prefer eventual consistency with versioned publishes and precomputed effective menus per store, so reads are fast and updates are safe to roll out.

creat displays for physical stores of a global fast food chain. each screen can have different ads, menu items.

prices and menu items vary by location

use intelligfence to push for smart choises like if it is a hot day push for cold beverages or if store is too full push for fast items like fries.

Mid November, 2025

Google

Staff

Design a system for Large fast food restaurent chain. Stores are locxated globally. Store can have diffrent menu based on location. Menu can have diffrent items. Items can have different price in different location. The menu will be shown on Panel in the restaurent. THe menu should be visually appealing. 

Late October, 2025

Google

Senior

Prompt;
A large multinational fast food company wants to design a system to display in store digital menus for each of their locations.
Content should be managed centrally potentially per country.
Not all locations have identical menu offerings.
The same item may have different prices at different locations.
Stores can have different numbers or arrangements of displays depending on the real estate footprint of the location.
The company needs to be able to schedule promotional pricing/sales that may be limited by time and/or region.
Displayed menus should be visually appealing and interesting.
The menus should include support for first-party advertising/promotional content in addition to the basic menu offerings.
This system should automatically feature breakfast, lunch, and dinner offerings at the appropriate time.
Menus should be able to highlight different items depending on dynamic conditions.
